threats.title: Governor execute() Allows Execution of Defeated and Expired Proposals
threats.hypothesis_description: Both Governor.sol and L2Governor.sol contain an execute() function that allows proposals in Succeeded, Defeated, OR Expired states to be executed. This means any proposal—even one overwhelmingly voted down—can have its actions executed, completely bypassing governance.
threats.investigation_rationale: The require statement explicitly includes Defeated and Expired: `require(status == ProposalState.Succeeded || status == ProposalState.Defeated || status == ProposalState.Expired)`. This is present in both Governor.sol and L2Governor.sol, meaning any concrete Governor implementation inheriting from either base is affected. An attacker can submit a malicious proposal, have it defeated by the community, and still execute it.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(status == ProposalState.Succeeded || status == ProposalState.Defeated || status == ProposalState.Expired) allows defeated proposals to execute

preconditions: A Governor contract is deployed using either Governor.sol or L2Governor.sol base
The Governor has authorization to perform meaningful actions (e.g., relay, governance-protected setters)
Attacker can submit a proposal (proposalThreshold returns 0 by default)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: execute() requires Succeeded || Defeated || Expired instead of only Succeeded
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Defeated proposals (voted down by community) are still executable, completely negating governance
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check restricting execution to only Succeeded state

analysis_questions: Which concrete Governor implementation is deployed and what actions can it perform?
Can the attacker meet proposalThreshold() to submit proposals?
What valuable actions are protected by onlyGovernance (relay, updateQuorumNumerator)?
validation_requirements: Verify execute() logic in deployed Governor contract
Identify all onlyGovernance-protected functions reachable via execute
Construct PoC: propose malicious action, vote against, execute anyway

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker submits proposal targeting relay() to drain governor funds or updateQuorumNumerator(0) to disable quorum. Community votes against. After voting period, attacker calls execute() and the malicious actions run because Defeated state is allowed.
prerequisites: Deployed Governor with meaningful governance powers

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete drain of Governor-held assets via relay(); permanent governance manipulation via quorum changes
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All governance participants and protocol assets under governance control
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None - executed proposals cannot be reversed

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests verifying that defeated proposals cannot be executed

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Standard gas costs for proposal + execute
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Two transactions: propose + wait for voting period + execute
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain but damage is done atomically in execute

threats.title: setRouter() Inverted Zero-Address Check Permanently Bricks Router Updates
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPoolManager.setRouter() has require(_router == address(0)) instead of require(_router != address(0)). This means the function only accepts address(0) as valid input. If called, it sets router to zero, causing all future launch() calls to revert. No valid router address can ever be set via this function.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code is demonstrably wrong. The require condition is inverted—it should reject zero addresses but instead requires them. If the owner calls setRouter(address(0)), the router becomes zero and all genesis pool launches will revert when calling IRouter(address(0)).addLiquidity(). The owner cannot fix this since setRouter only accepts address(0).

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_router == address(0), 'ZA') should be != instead of ==

preconditions: Owner attempts to update router address via setRouter()
Current router address was set during initialize()

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Only address(0) passes the check, setting router to zero bricks all launches
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Correct check should be require(_router != address(0))

analysis_questions: Is the router currently set to a valid address from initialize()?
Are there other mechanisms to update the router besides setRouter()?
If setRouter(address(0)) is called, can any genesis pool ever launch?
validation_requirements: Verify that calling setRouter with a valid address reverts
Verify that calling setRouter(address(0)) succeeds and bricks launches
Check if there's an alternative path to update the router

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If owner ever calls setRouter(address(0)) (perhaps trying to test or misunderstanding the code), all genesis pool launches permanently fail. There is no way to set a valid router address through this function.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All genesis pool launches fail permanently; native tokens and funding deposits locked
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All genesis pool participants and token owners
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Only via contract upgrade (GenesisPoolManager is upgradeable)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for setRouter with valid address

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Would be caught on first attempted router update

threats.title: GenesisPoolManager.depositToken() Accepts Arbitrary Unvalidated Contract as GenesisPool
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPoolManager.depositToken() accepts any address as the genesisPool parameter without verifying it was created by the GenesisPoolFactory. An attacker can deploy a malicious contract implementing the IGenesisPool interface that returns true from depositToken(), triggering _preLaunchPool() which creates a gauge via gaugeManager.createGauge() for an attacker-controlled pair address.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function only checks genesisPool != address(0) and amount > 0, then directly calls IGenesisPool(genesisPool).depositToken(msg.sender, amount). If the return value is true, it calls _preLaunchPool which creates a gauge and transfers incentives. The attacker's malicious contract controls all return values including getLiquidityPoolInfo().pairAddress.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No validation that genesisPool was created by genesisFactory; directly calls IGenesisPool(genesisPool).depositToken()

preconditions: Attacker can deploy a contract implementing IGenesisPool interface
gaugeManager.createGauge() succeeds for the attacker's pair address
The fake genesisPool's depositToken returns true

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No factory validation: require(genesisPool != address(0)) is the only check on the address
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Malicious contract can return arbitrary values from IGenesisPool interface functions

analysis_questions: Does gaugeManager.createGauge() validate the pool address against the pair factory?
What happens when a gauge is created for an unauthorized pair?
Can this be used to redirect emissions to attacker-controlled pools?
validation_requirements: Deploy a mock IGenesisPool that returns true from depositToken and attacker-controlled pair from getLiquidityPoolInfo
Verify that _preLaunchPool successfully creates a gauge for the fake pair
Assess impact: can the fake gauge receive emissions?

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys FakeGenesisPool that: depositToken() transfers no tokens but returns true, getGenesisInfo().nativeToken returns attacker's token, getLiquidityPoolInfo().pairAddress returns attacker's pair. Attacker calls depositToken(fakePool, 1). Manager calls _preLaunchPool creating a gauge for attacker's pair, potentially enabling emissions theft.
prerequisites: gaugeManager doesn't independently validate pair legitimacy

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Unauthorized gauge creation redirecting protocol emissions; potential whitelisting of arbitrary tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol stakeholders via diluted emissions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Governance could remove the unauthorized gauge

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for depositToken with non-factory genesisPool address

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: ~500K gas for contract deployment + depositToken call
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Single transaction after deploying fake contract
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain visible but damage may be done before detection

threats.title: Thenian mintPublic Reentrancy via _safeMint Bypasses Per-Wallet NFT Limit
threats.hypothesis_description: Thenian.mintPublic() uses _safeMint which triggers onERC721Received callback on contract recipients. The per-wallet limit check (balanceOf + amount <= 15) occurs only at function entry. During _safeMint's callback, an attacker contract can re-enter mintPublic with a new amount that passes the stale balanceOf check, then the outer loop continues minting beyond the 15-token limit.
threats.investigation_rationale: mintPublic checks balanceOf(msg.sender).add(amount) <= 15 at entry, then enters a loop calling _safeMint. After the first _safeMint, balanceOf is 1, but the outer loop expects to mint 9 more. If the attacker re-enters with amount=10 during the callback (check: 1+10=11 <= 15 passes), the inner call mints 10 tokens (balanceOf becomes 11), then the outer loop continues minting 9 more, reaching balanceOf=20 > 15.

proof_of_signal.pattern: balanceOf check at entry, _safeMint in loop enables reentrancy; no nonReentrant guard

preconditions: Public sale period is active (timestamp within 2-5 days after SALE_START_TIMESTAMP)
Attacker deploys a contract that implements onERC721Received to re-enter mintPublic
totalSupply() + total minted < MAX_SUPPLY
Attacker provides sufficient ETH/AVAX for all NFT payments

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Per-wallet limit checked once at entry; _safeMint callback allows re-entry before loop completes
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After re-entry mints additional tokens, outer loop continues minting, total exceeds 15 per wallet
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No nonReentrant modifier on mintPublic; no per-iteration balance check

analysis_questions: Does _safeMint update balanceOf before the callback (yes, standard OZ behavior)?
How many total NFTs can be minted via nested reentrancy?
Does this impact mintFirst or mintSecond as well?
validation_requirements: Deploy attacker contract that re-enters mintPublic in onERC721Received
Verify final balanceOf exceeds 15 for the attacker contract
Calculate maximum mintable via deep reentrancy chains

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker contract calls mintPublic(10) with 10*NFT_PRICE. During first _safeMint callback, re-enters mintPublic(10) with 10*NFT_PRICE. Inner check: balanceOf(1)+10=11<=15 passes. Inner mints 10. Outer resumes minting 9 more. Final: 20 NFTs for one wallet, bypassing 15-per-wallet limit. Deeper reentrancy can push this further.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Attacker mints more NFTs than the 15-per-wallet limit allows, violating scarcity guarantees
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All NFT holders (diluted relative scarcity per intended distribution)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No reentrancy tests for mintPublic via contract callers

threats.title: Governor.sol _voteSucceeded Requires forVotes > abstainVotes Enabling Abstain-Based Blocking
threats.hypothesis_description: In Governor.sol's L2GovernorCountingSimple, _voteSucceeded requires both forVotes > againstVotes AND forVotes > abstainVotes. Combined with the execute() bug (TECH-H001), proposals where forVotes > againstVotes but forVotes < abstainVotes fall into Expired state and are still executable. Strategically, an attacker can use abstain votes to push proposals into this ambiguous state.
threats.investigation_rationale: The L2GovernorCountingSimple in Governor.sol defines _voteSucceeded as: forVotes > againstVotes && forVotes > abstainVotes. The standalone L2GovernorCountingSimple.sol only requires forVotes > againstVotes. The Governor.sol version means abstain votes can prevent a proposal from being Succeeded while not making it Defeated either, landing in Expired state which is still executable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _voteSucceeded requires forVotes > againstVotes && forVotes > abstainVotes; inconsistent with standard governance semantics

preconditions: Governor.sol variant is the deployed Governor implementation
Proposal reaches quorum
Abstain votes exceed for votes

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: forVotes > againstVotes && forVotes > abstainVotes — abstain acts as blocking vote
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Proposal with majority For (over Against) enters Expired due to high Abstain, yet is still executable via TECH-H001
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Standard pattern only requires forVotes > againstVotes for success

analysis_questions: Which Governor variant is actually deployed?
Can abstain votes be weaponized to force desired proposals into Expired state?
Combined with TECH-H001, does this create an attack where attacker forces Expired then executes?
validation_requirements: Verify vote counting logic produces Expired for scenarios with high abstain
Confirm execute() allows Expired proposals
Test scenario: proposal with 100 for, 50 against, 150 abstain

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Legitimate proposal has 100 for, 50 against. Attacker casts 150 abstain votes. Quorum reached, but _voteSucceeded returns false (100 < 150 abstain) and _voteDefeated returns false (50 < 100). State becomes Expired. With TECH-H001, Expired proposals can still be executed, creating confusion about governance legitimacy.
prerequisites: Sufficient voting power for abstain block

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Governance outcomes become unpredictable; proposals execute regardless of vote outcome

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for abstain-dominated vote scenarios

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Sufficient smNFT voting power for abstain votes

threats.title: Zero Slippage Protection in Genesis Pool Launch Enables Liquidity Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPool._addLiquidityAndDistribute() calls IRouter.addLiquidity with amountAMin=0 and amountBMin=0. An attacker can pre-seed the pair with tokens (by direct ERC20 transfer) before the predictable epoch-triggered launch, causing the addLiquidity to accept a manipulated ratio and extract value via subsequent arbitrage.
threats.investigation_rationale: The pair address is known after approveGenesisPool creates/assigns it. The launch timing is predictable (epoch flip). If the pair's genesis status doesn't prevent token balance manipulation, an attacker can transfer tokens to the pair before launch, skewing reserves. With zero min amounts, the router accepts any ratio.
preconditions: Pair address is created and known (after approveGenesisPool)
Genesis status on pair does not prevent direct ERC20 transfers + reserve manipulation
Attacker can observe the pool is in PRE_LAUNCH_DEPOSIT_DISABLED state (predictable launch)
Attacker has tokens to pre-seed the pair

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: addLiquidity called with 0, 0 for min amounts
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No slippage protection means any reserve ratio is accepted
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should compute min amounts as percentage of desired amounts

analysis_questions: Does setGenesisStatus(true) on the pair prevent direct token transfers or sync/mint calls?
Can an attacker call pair.sync() or pair.mint() while genesis status is true?
What is the economic profit from skewing the liquidity ratio?
validation_requirements: Examine pair factory's setGenesisStatus behavior (external contract)
Test: transfer tokens to genesis-status pair, then trigger launch
Calculate attacker profit from ratio manipulation

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker observes pool in PRE_LAUNCH_DEPOSIT_DISABLED. Transfers tokens directly to pair contract. On epoch flip, launch() calls addLiquidity with 0 slippage, accepting skewed ratio. Fewer LP tokens minted than expected. Attacker arbitrages the mispriced pool, extracting value from genesis depositors.
prerequisites: Pair genesis status doesn't block token balance manipulation

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Significant value extraction from genesis pool depositors via manipulated liquidity ratio
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All genesis pool depositors and token owner
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Cannot be reversed after liquidity is added

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for addLiquidity with pre-existing pair reserves

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Depends on pool size; needs enough to skew ratio meaningfully
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-block: pre-seed before epoch flip, arbitrage after

threats.title: Governor.sol Quorum Uses Current Total Supply Instead of Snapshot Supply
threats.hypothesis_description: In Governor.sol's L2GovernorVotesQuorumFraction, the quorum() function calls token.getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() without passing the blockTimestamp parameter, meaning quorum is calculated against the current total supply rather than the supply at the proposal's snapshot time. This allows quorum manipulation after proposal creation.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function signature accepts blockTimestamp but ignores it: `return (token.getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() * quorumNumerator()) / quorumDenominator()`. Compare with L2GovernorVotesQuorumFraction.sol which correctly uses: `return (token.getPastTotalSupply(blockTimestamp) * quorumNumerator()) / quorumDenominator()`. This means quorum threshold changes with current supply, not snapshot supply.

proof_of_signal.pattern: token.getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() called without blockTimestamp parameter; ignores function argument

preconditions: Governor.sol variant is deployed
smNFT total supply changes between proposal creation and state evaluation
Attacker or external events can influence smNFT total supply

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() without timestamp vs L2GovernorVotesQuorumFraction.sol's getPastTotalSupply(blockTimestamp)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Quorum threshold is dynamic post-proposal, allowing retroactive manipulation

analysis_questions: Which Governor variant is deployed?
Can smNFT total supply be influenced by any actor?
What is the practical magnitude of supply changes between proposal and execution?
validation_requirements: Verify quorum returns different values at different total supply levels for the same proposalId
Test: create proposal, change supply, verify quorum changes retroactively
Assess whether supply changes are frequent enough to exploit

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker creates a malicious proposal when smNFT supply is low (easy quorum). A few supporters vote for it. Before execution, total supply is checked at current value. If supply decreased further, quorum is even easier. If supply increased, attacker could strategically time the execution window.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Governance proposals pass or fail based on manipulated quorum thresholds

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying quorum uses snapshot supply

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Sufficient smNFTs to influence supply or meet quorum
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-block between proposal creation and voting deadline
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Quorum changes visible via quorum() queries

threats.title: Incentives Mapping Not Zeroed After transferIncentives Causes Irrecoverable Funds Display
threats.hypothesis_description: After transferIncentives() sends incentive tokens to the external bribe, the incentives mapping retains non-zero values. If the pool later transitions to NOT_QUALIFIED (via setPoolStatus), claimIncentives() will attempt to transfer tokens the pool no longer holds, reverting via SafeERC20 and permanently preventing any incentive claims.
threats.investigation_rationale: transferIncentives() approves and sends tokens to external_bribe but never sets incentives[token] = 0. The _setPoolStatus function allows transitioning to NOT_QUALIFIED from any state when called by manager. In NOT_QUALIFIED state, claimIncentives() iterates incentiveTokens and tries to transfer incentives[token] amounts, but the balance is zero, causing permanent revert.

proof_of_signal.pattern: transferIncentives sends tokens via notifyRewardAmount but never zeros incentives[token]

preconditions: Genesis pool has incentives added
Pool reaches PRE_LAUNCH state (transferIncentives called)
Manager calls setPoolStatus(NOT_QUALIFIED) after incentive transfer

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: incentives[token] never zeroed in transferIncentives; claimIncentives reads stale non-zero values
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Token owner sees claimable incentives but transfer reverts due to zero balance
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: transferIncentives should set incentives[incentiveTokens[i]] = 0 after sending

analysis_questions: Can manager set pool to NOT_QUALIFIED after PRE_LAUNCH?
Is there any path to recover incentive tokens from external bribe?
Does claimableIncentives mislead the tokenOwner?
validation_requirements: Trace: addIncentives -> transferIncentives -> setPoolStatus(NOT_QUALIFIED) -> claimIncentives
Verify claimIncentives reverts when balance is zero
Check if external bribe has recovery mechanism

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Token owner adds incentives. Pool progresses to PRE_LAUNCH, incentives sent to bribe. Manager sets NOT_QUALIFIED (e.g., external issue). Token owner calls claimIncentives, transaction reverts because pool has no incentive token balance. Incentive tokens are unrecoverable.
prerequisites: Pool transitions back to NOT_QUALIFIED after incentive transfer

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Token owner permanently loses all incentive tokens deposited

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for claim after transfer + status change

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: N/A — affects token owner's deposited incentives
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Token owner discovers when trying to claim

threats.title: DoS in checkAtEpochFlip/checkBeforeEpochFlip if Any Single Pool Reverts
threats.hypothesis_description: checkAtEpochFlip() and checkBeforeEpochFlip() iterate over all liveNativeTokens and perform external calls for each pool. If any single pool's operation reverts (e.g., due to a broken genesis pool, failed pair factory call, or malicious token), the entire epoch flip transaction reverts, blocking all other pools from progressing.
threats.investigation_rationale: Both functions loop over liveNativeTokens and make multiple external calls per pool: getGenesisPool, poolStatus, eligbleForPreLaunchPool/eligbleForDisqualify, setPoolStatus, launch, etc. There is no try-catch wrapping individual pool operations. One revert blocks all pools and prevents epoch state transitions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Loop with multiple external calls per iteration; no try-catch; single revert blocks all

preconditions: Multiple genesis pools are live
One pool has a condition that causes external call to revert (e.g., malicious token, broken pair factory interaction)
epochController calls checkAtEpochFlip or checkBeforeEpochFlip

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unbounded loop with external calls, no try-catch isolation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Single pool failure blocks all other pools in same epoch
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use try-catch around individual pool operations

analysis_questions: What external calls in the loop can revert?
Can a malicious token owner deliberately cause reverts to block other pools?
Does pre_epoch_period update get blocked too?
validation_requirements: Identify all revert-capable external calls in the loop
Test: add a pool with a reverting token/pair, verify other pools are blocked
Verify pre_epoch_period is not updated on revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker's genesis pool has a native token that reverts on balanceOf or other calls made during launch. When epochController calls checkAtEpochFlip, the attacker's pool causes a revert, blocking all other legitimate pools from launching. This persists until the problematic pool is manually removed.
prerequisites: Attacker has a whitelisted and approved genesis pool
Pool causes revert during epoch processing

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All genesis pools stuck in limbo; deposits locked past intended timeline
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors and token owners of other genesis pools
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Governance can reject the problematic pool, but requires manual intervention

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for epoch flip with failing pool in the batch

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Gas limit for large liveNativeTokens arrays
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Need whitelisted genesis pool (requires governance cooperation or compromise)
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Epoch flip failures are immediately visible

threats.title: deductAmount Arithmetic May Underflow for TokenOwner Who Is Also a Depositor
threats.hypothesis_description: In GenesisPool.deductAmount(), when account is the tokenOwner, the else branch computes userAmount -= (totalDeposits * pendingOwnerStaked) / _depositerLiquidity. Due to integer division rounding, this subtracted value may be less than expected, leaving userAmount larger than userDeposits[account], causing underflow revert and permanently locking the tokenOwner's LP tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function first computes userAmount = (totalDeposits * gaugeTokenAmount) / _depositerLiquidity. For tokenOwner in the else branch (gaugeTokenAmount >= pendingOwnerStaked), it subtracts: userAmount -= (totalDeposits * pendingOwnerStaked) / _depositerLiquidity. Due to truncation in both divisions, the net userAmount may exceed userDeposits[account], causing the final subtraction to underflow.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Two integer divisions with different numerators can produce rounding asymmetry; final subtraction from userDeposits may underflow

preconditions: tokenOwner is also a depositor (has non-zero userDeposits)
Gauge calls deductAmount with gaugeTokenAmount >= pendingOwnerStaked
Integer division rounding causes userAmount > userDeposits[tokenOwner]

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: userAmount = A/B; userAmount -= C/B; then userDeposits -= userAmount; rounding in A/B and C/B creates asymmetry
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: (A/B - C/B) != ((A-C)/B) due to independent truncation; result may exceed actual user deposits
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No min(userAmount, userDeposits[account]) check before final subtraction

analysis_questions: What values of liquidity, totalDeposits, and gaugeTokenAmount produce underflow?
Is the tokenOwner likely to also be a depositor?
Does this permanently lock their LP tokens in the gauge?
validation_requirements: Find concrete numeric examples where underflow occurs
Test with edge case: odd liquidity, small deposits, specific gaugeTokenAmount
Verify that underflow causes permanent lock (no alternative withdrawal path)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: TokenOwner also deposits funding tokens. After launch, they try to unstake from gauge. Gauge calls deductAmount with specific gaugeTokenAmount. Integer division rounding causes userAmount to be 1 wei more than userDeposits[tokenOwner]. Transaction reverts with underflow. TokenOwner's LP tokens are permanently locked.
prerequisites: Specific numeric conditions for rounding asymmetry

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: TokenOwner's LP tokens permanently locked in gauge
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Token owner who is also a depositor
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None without contract upgrade; deductAllAmount might work as alternative

threats.test_coverage_gap: No edge-case arithmetic tests for deductAmount with tokenOwner as depositor

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Discovered when tokenOwner tries to unstake

threats.title: Auction Address Can Be Set to Zero via Removed AuctionFactory Entries
threats.hypothesis_description: In GenesisPoolManager.depositNativeToken(), the auction is selected via auctionFactory.auctions(auctionIndex) with a fallback to auctions(0). If both the requested index and index 0 point to removed (zeroed) entries, the auction is set to address(0) in the genesis pool, causing all subsequent depositToken calls to revert when calling auction methods.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code: `auction = auctionFactory.auctions(auctionIndex); auction = auction == address(0) ? auctionFactory.auctions(0) : auction;`. If auctionFactory.removeAuction(0) was called, auctions[0] = address(0). Then both branches return address(0). setGenesisPoolInfo stores address(0) as the auction, and _getNativeTokenAmount will revert on any call to IAuction(address(0)).
deployment_assumptions: AuctionFactory owner removes the auction at index 0
threats.config_evidence: AuctionFactory.removeAuction() sets auctions[_pos] = address(0); no deployment evidence prevents removal of index 0

proof_of_signal.pattern: auction fallback to auctions(0) which can be address(0) if removed; no null check before setGenesisPoolInfo

preconditions: AuctionFactory owner removes the default auction at index 0
User calls depositNativeToken with an invalid or removed auctionIndex

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No null check on final auction address before use
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: address(0) stored as auction, bricking all deposit operations

analysis_questions: Is index 0 the only fallback? Could another fallback mechanism exist?
Can governance/admin recover by calling setAuction on the affected pool?
What's the blast radius: just one pool or all future pools?
validation_requirements: Remove auction at index 0, then call depositNativeToken
Verify genesis pool stores address(0) as auction
Verify subsequent depositToken calls revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Admin removes default auction. User deposits native token. Auction set to address(0). All user deposit attempts on this genesis pool revert. Pool becomes permanently non-functional unless governance calls setAuction to fix it.
prerequisites: User creates genesis pool during this window

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Genesis pool bricked; native tokens locked unless pool is rejected
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Token owner and potential depositors of the affected pool
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Governance can call setAuction() to fix the pool

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for removed auction fallback behavior
